source.title: Hippocampal Remapping as Hidden State Inference

confidence_rationale: Sanders, Wilson & Gershman (2020) provide a Bayesian computational framework for hippocampal remapping that directly supports the ARC-042 staged developmental account. The paper's core claim is that hippocampal context representations are not raw perceptual encodings of environmental features but are posterior beliefs about hidden states generating the observations. Remapping (a change in which hippocampal map is active) reflects Bayesian inference over hidden states rather than direct response to sensory change. The model uses a Chinese Restaurant Process (CRP) prior over hidden states, parametrized by alpha (the tendency to create new states vs. assign to existing ones). Key relevance to ARC-042: (1) The framework formalizes why an untrained or bootstrapping-phase ContextMemory cannot discriminate contexts -- without a prior over hidden states that has been updated by sufficient experience, the posterior is flat (all contexts appear equally probable) and the system defaults to a single attractor (all contexts map to the same representation). (2) The staged Phase 0/1/2 structure of ARC-042 maps onto the development of the prior: Phase 0 is the bootstrap epoch during which the agent lacks an informative context prior; Phase 1 (supervised training) is the process by which the CRP prior is shaped by labeled experience into a distribution that can discriminate terrain types; Phase 2 uses the trained prior for inference. (3) The paper makes a specific prediction that directly validates ARC-042's Phase 1 gate: the quality of context inference is bounded by the quality of the generative model (prior + likelihood). Before the prior is trained, no amount of evidence at inference time is sufficient to produce discriminating representations. This is the functional analog of the Phase 1 gate in ARC-042: ContextMemory is not useful for context-gated evaluation until Phase 1 has trained the hidden-state prior sufficiently. Confidence at 0.73: the Bayesian framework is rigorous and the mapping is conceptually tight, but the CRP prior is a discrete generative model over a fixed observation space, not a learned LSTM representation; transfer requires interpretation rather than direct mapping.
failure_signatures: The CRP-based model uses a non-parametric Bayesian framework with analytically tractable posteriors. The ContextMemory module uses learned distributed representations (LSTM activations) without an explicit generative model. The inference mechanism in Sanders et al. (Bayesian posterior update) is not the same as the write/retrieve dynamics of a differentiable associative memory.

Sanders et al. model remapping as a discrete state-transition event (switch hidden state vs. stay in current state). ARC-042's ContextMemory uses continuous context vectors -- the 'remapping' equivalent is a change in the cosine similarity of retrieved vectors, not a discrete state flip.

The paper focuses on what determines WHEN remapping occurs (the Bayesian decision boundary). ARC-042's Phase 1 addresses HOW discriminating representations are learned, not when switching occurs. These are complementary but distinct questions.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Hippocampal remapping is hidden state inference: place cells do not directly encode sensory features but maintain beliefs about latent environmental states. The system inverts a generative model (observations generated by hidden states drawn from a CRP prior) to compute posterior beliefs. Quality of context discrimination is bounded by the quality of the prior -- a flat prior (no learned context structure) cannot support context discrimination regardless of observation quality.
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-042's Phase 1 gate is the process of learning an informative prior over terrain contexts. During Phase 0, ContextMemory has a flat prior (no trained hidden-state prior) -- this corresponds to the 'new environment' regime in Sanders et al. where all observations are equally likely to be from a new state. Phase 1 (supervised terrain-label training) updates the prior to discriminate hazard-proximate from hazard-distal states -- this corresponds to the CRP prior being shaped by multi-context experience. Phase 2 then uses this trained prior for inference. The Sanders et al. framework predicts that Phase 2 context gating cannot work before Phase 1: without an informative prior, posterior beliefs are underdetermined and context representations are undifferentiated (analogous to cosine_sim=1.0 in EXQ-187a). The paper thus provides Bayesian grounding for the Phase 1 gate as a necessary computational requirement, not merely a training-schedule convenience.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Sanders et al. model discrete state inference with a parametric generative model. ContextMemory uses learned continuous representations. The Bayesian framing provides conceptual support for the Phase 1 gate but does not translate into implementation-level predictions about lambda, episode counts, or LSTM capacity.
mapping.source_context: Computational model of CA3/CA1 remapping in rodents; validated against place cell recording data; uses Chinese Restaurant Process for non-parametric context inference

confidence_components.notes: eLife -- top-tier open-access neuroscience journal; Sanders, Wilson & Gershman are leading researchers in computational neuroscience and reinforcement learning. Mapping fidelity moderate-high: the Bayesian prior-learning framing maps onto ARC-042's staged training structure well. Transfer risk moderate: discrete CRP inference to continuous LSTM representation learning requires interpretive bridging.
